what_it_does: Records a design note that diagnoses why comments get dropped and proposes concrete routing patterns: a per-envelope assignee with a status lifecycle, in-context reply badges, and structured option-set questions back to a decision-maker.
observations: Item is part_of board://item-64466290 and labeled P9 section 8.
Title states the hard lesson: an unassigned comment is a dropped comment.
Proposes a status lifecycle: received → triaged → working → replied → applied/closed.
Replies should appear both in an inbox overlay (V/radial) and as a badge on the anchor block/element.
Member questions to Tim should be rendered as typed option-sets/tap-targets, not free text.
Cites Linear, GitHub, Asana, Notion, and Medium as convergent evidence.
standouts: The core maxim: an unassigned comment is a dropped comment.
Explicit per-envelope assignee plus a visible five-state lifecycle.
Two-surface reply visibility: inbox overlay plus anchor badge.
Generalizing the GitHub suggested-change accept loop into structured tap-target decisions.
conventions: Uses YAML frontmatter with id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, links, created/updated, and history.
Markdown body with a numbered section heading and bullet recommendations.
References external product behaviors as evidence.
No code symbols or implementation details.
concerns: Terms like Tim, the brief, envelopes, and V/radial overlay are used without definition in this file.
Assumes prior knowledge of P9 and parent item-64466290.
Does not specify how status transitions are triggered or who can change them.
No acceptance criteria or examples of the structured option-set UI.
notes: A design note arguing that actionable comments need a single owner and a visible lifecycle, with replies shown in context and decisions returned as structured choices.
questions: Who or what is Tim in this system?
What is the V/radial overlay and how is it rendered?
What is the relationship between a brief, an envelope, a comment, and a block/element anchor?
How is the assignee chosen and how do status transitions occur?
What happens when a comment is not actionable or requires multiple owners?
purpose_vs_actual: Yes — the title frames it as a lesson about routing to people who act, and the body turns that lesson into concrete patterns for assignment, status, contextual surfacing, and structured decisions.
apparent_themes: Tool convergence implies a durable pattern.
intention_signals: It is trying to persuade the design to adopt Linear/GitHub-style ownership and lifecycle discipline for comments, while keeping the user experience anchored in the original block and minimizing prose parsing through structured option-sets.
novelty: It generalizes the GitHub suggested-change accept loop into a reusable UI pattern for any member-to-Tim decision, paired with a two-surface reply notification model.
contribution: The idea that every actionable comment must have one assignee and one status, plus the pattern of showing replies both in an inbox and at their anchor, and the notion of returning decisions as structured option-sets rather than free-text questions.
summary_for_embedding: A noticeboard design note proposing that comments require a single assignee and a visible status lifecycle, with replies surfaced both in an inbox overlay and as badges on their anchor blocks, and member questions returned as structured tap-target options.
intention_for_embedding: To prevent actionable comments from silently dying by introducing ownership, lifecycle tracking, contextual reply surfacing, and structured decision loops.
